coredata.dc:title: Amino acid-based nanoparticles-incorporated thin-film nanocomposite forward osmosis membranes for efficient desalination and heavy metal ions rejection

coredata.dc:description: The current study explores potential applications of state-of-the-art thin-film nanocomposite forward osmosis (TFN-FO) membranes, modified with histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) and MIP-202(Zr) nanoparticles (NPs), for sustainable desalination and heavy metal ions rejection. The porous and layered structure of the applied NPs, along with various hydrophilic functional groups on their surface, contribute to improving the fabricated membranes' ion/water separation performance. The successful preparation and incorporation of desired NPs into the polyamide layer was investigated using typical analytical methods. Under the common FO test conditions, the best-performing TFN-MQ2 membrane displayed a water flux of 21.8 LMH, which was over 1.5 times greater than the water flux of blank TFC. Simultaneously, the selectivity was found to be approximately 1.7 times greater than that of the unmodified TFC membrane. Moreover, the optimal TFN-MQ2 membrane exhibited superior rejection rates for Cu2+ ions (98.5 %) and Pb2+ ions (98.1 %), surpassing all other samples in heavy metal ion rejection. The findings of this study suggest that carefully choosing cost-efficient and eco-friendly nanofillers (such as amino acid-based NPs) can enhance the desalination performance of TFN-FO membranes and bolster their resistance to fouling and rejection of heavy metal ions. Not to mention, the overall costs of membrane production will be reduced.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study explores potential applications of state-of-the-art thin-film nanocomposite forward osmosis (TFN-FO) membranes, modified with histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) and MIP-202(Zr) nanoparticles (NPs), for sustainable desalination and heavy metal ions rejection. The porous and layered structure of the applied NPs, along with various hydrophilic functional groups on their surface, contribute to improving the fabricated membranes' ion/water separation performance. The successful preparation and incorporation of desired NPs into the polyamide layer was investigated using typical analytical methods. Under the common FO test conditions, the best-performing TFN-MQ2 membrane displayed a water flux of 21.8 LMH, which was over 1.5 times greater than the water flux of blank TFC. Simultaneously, the selectivity was found to be approximately 1.7 times greater than that of the unmodified TFC membrane. Moreover, the optimal TFN-MQ2 membrane exhibited superior rejection rates for Cu2+ ions (98.5 %) and Pb2+ ions (98.1 %), surpassing all other samples in heavy metal ion rejection. The findings of this study suggest that carefully choosing cost-efficient and eco-friendly nanofillers (such as amino acid-based NPs) can enhance the desalination performance of TFN-FO membranes and bolster their resistance to fouling and rejection of heavy metal ions. Not to mention, the overall costs of membrane production will be reduced.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and its supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Water shortage and contamination of surface and groundwater sources due to overpopulation, climate change, endless and uninterrupted activities of industry, mining, agriculture, and urbanization activities pose significant challenges for human life now and in the future [1,2]. Given the inaccessibility to adequate safe potable water and the necessity to eliminate heavy metal (HM) ions from wastewater prior to disposal, communities have been inevitably pushed towards treating salty and contaminated water [3–6]. HM ions such as Hg(II), Cd(II), Cr(III), Cr(VI), Pb(II), As(III), Cu(II), Ni(II), and Zn(II) are among the most hazardous types of pollutants found in aquatic environments [7–9]. The infiltration of HMs pollutants into water sources is primarily due to erosion and weathering, the discharging of untreated or insufficiently treated industrial effluents (such as from electroplating, batteries, and pesticides), and the disposal of domestic sewage into rivers, along with the use of certain metal salts in agriculture [10–15]. Surprisingly, these often toxic metals are non-biodegradable and could adversely impact human health [7,16].

Various methods have been implemented so far to treat waters contaminated with heavy metals, such as adsorption, chemical precipitation, coagulation/flocculation, and ion exchange [17–19]. However, these techniques fall short of achieving the high retention rate mandated by the standard guidelines set by the World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and have their own limitations. The expense of chemical precipitation and flotation typically tends to be high, with additional costs involved in disposing of excess toxic chemical sludge produced by these methods [20,21]. Ion exchange is unable to manage concentrated metal solutions because it becomes clogged by organic matter and other solids present in the wastewater. Additionally, ion exchange is not selective and is greatly influenced by the pH of the solution [22]. Although adsorption has the benefits of being inexpensive and fast due to the numerous low-cost adsorbents available, adsorption is hindered by its lack of selectivity and lengthy regeneration process [23,24]. More recently, several membrane-based processes namely UF, NF, RO, and FO have been employed to address this limitation, leading to a substantial increase in the number of studies [25–28]. However, the rapid penetration decreases because of suspended particles and irreversible fouling in pressure-driven membrane technologies (i.e., UF, NF, and RO), raises operational expenses from frequent membrane replacements. Besides, an additional purification step is necessary since UF and NF processes do not adequately remove HM ions during water treatment, resulting in additional expenses [26]. Hence, pressure-driven membrane processes are seen as costly and energy-intensive processes, with low water recovery rates and facing the risk of severe membrane fouling. The FO process as a suitable alternative process has emerged as a promising method thanks to its cost-effectiveness, effortless performance, higher water recovery rate, and lower fouling tendency compared to other traditional pressure-driven membrane processes [29,30]. To summarize, throughout the FO process, the difference in osmotic pressure between draw and feed solutions (DS and FS) drives the separation of water from FS, which is characterized by low osmotic pressure compared to its counterpart [30,31]. Although the FO process holds great promise, its membranes still face some challenges, such as low water flux and selectivity, along with fouling that can drastically restrict the membrane's overall function. To tackle the underlying reason for the low water flux of FO membranes, it is crucial to reduce internal concentration polarization (ICP) caused by the irreversible dilution of a draw solution with a permeate solution. Choosing appropriate design factors for the membrane can help alleviate ICP. In this aspect, a thin-film composite (TFC) membrane, known as a key asset in membrane desalination, is expected to ease the ICP and the subsequent decrease in the osmotic driving force for water permeation due to its support layer's porosity [32,33]. For further improvement in desalination efficiency, numerous researchers have suggested different approaches to enhance the permeability and selectivity of TFC-FO membranes. Surface modification of TFC membranes by nanomaterials and different chemicals is one of these feasible approaches [34–36]. Many studies exist regarding the incorporation of nanoparticles (NPs) into the active polyamide (PA) layer of TFC membranes, with a preference for NPs that contain organic components in their structure like MOFs and GO over pure inorganic compounds [37–40]. One of the primary reasons why these compounds are favored is their desirable compatibility with the polymer matrix, which reduces the loss probability of NPs in the long run. Stability in the aquatic environment and the ability to adjust the pore size (for hydrated ions rejection) are additional factors that contribute to MOFs being a promising material choice. However, the majority of MOFs are synthesized using toxic and costly starting materials (metal sources, linkers, and solvents), posing a risk to both the environment and human health, and hindering the commercialization of the final product (MOFs-incorporated membranes) [38,41–46]. MIP-202(Zr), a water and chemically stable MOF, is made from natural amino acids and produced in eco-friendly water. It has the ability to be easily produced on a larger scale because of its gentle reaction conditions and the use of low-cost and non-toxic linker material (l-aspartic acid) [47,48].

Carbon-based nanomaterials like GO and carbon/graphene quantum dots (C/GQDs) are other on-the-table compounds currently used in membrane modification. These additives are incorporated into the selective polyamide layer of TFC-FO membranes to enhance their thermal stability, mechanical strength, fouling resistance, chlorine resistance, and separation efficiency [49–51]. The core benefits of these compounds are layered structure, organic nature, richness in hydrophilic functional groups (hydroxyl and acidic), and low-cost and eco-friendly preparation procedures. Functionalized GQDs containing different functional groups have demonstrated impressive outcomes in metal ions adsorption capacity, leading to their use in metal ions detection and sensing applications [52,53]. Modifying GQDs with amine functional groups and imidazole rings greatly enhances their capability to adsorb and detect divalent HM ions, particularly Cu(II), due to including nitrogen atoms in their composition [54,55]. On that account, introducing these highly hydrophilic, stable, and environmentally friendly NPs into the active layer of the FO membrane can improve both water flux and resistance to fouling, as well as boost its ability to heavy metal ions rejection.

To our knowledge, no report in the literature has investigated the modification of the TFC-FO membranes by applying inexpensive and environmentally friendly MOFs and functionalized GQDs. This paper explores the science behind exploiting the specific properties of MIP-202(Zr) and histidine-functionalized GQDs (His-GQDs) in enhancing the desalination, antifouling, and HM ions rejection performance of developed NPs-PA/substrate FO membranes. These capabilities are enabled by specific characteristics such as the additives' hydrophilic nature, newly formed selective channels within the active layer (i.e., MOF pores with smaller window size than hydrated salt ions, ∼ 4–5.5 Å, and channels formed between NPs and PA layer), adsorption of HM ions by nanofillers' functional groups, and electrostatic repulsion interactions between the MOF linker's NH3+ group and ions with the same charge [47]. One must bear in mind that the functional groups' interactions between nanofillers and monomers (trimesoyl chloride and m-phenylenediamine) during interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction can be involved in and improve NPs stability on the membrane surface (PA layer). The intended NPs were simultaneously dispersed in the aqueous monomer solution in order to be embedded in the rejection layer of PA-based FO membranes. Having evaluated the successful formation of the PA layer and nanofillers incorporation using routine characterization analysis, the efficiency of the fabricated membranes in the desalination and HMs rejection process was investigated. Moreover, the antifouling feature of the membranes was assessed when exposed to bovine serum albumin (BSA) contamination in the FS.

All of the used materials and analysis instruments are mentioned in the Supplementary Information (SI) file. MIP-202 NPs were prepared according to the previously reported hydrothermal procedure with some alteration [48]. In short, in a 25 mL round bottom flask, 10 mmol of L-Asp was dispersed in 5 mL deionized water (DI) by gently stirring for 15 min. Next, 10 mmol of ZrCl4 was added to the above mixture in multiple steps to form a colorless and transparent solution. The inner face of the flask was washed with an additional 5 mL of DI water to rinse the adhered ZrCl4 powder into the solution. The resultant solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. After collecting the white precipitate by centrifugation, the residue was washed three times with water/EtOH at room temperature. The dried product (at 80 °C overnight) was pulverized, and the resulting powder was labeled MIP-202 seed before being used. Afterward, 0.1 g of the as-prepared MIP-202 seeds and 20 mmol of L-Asp were dispersed in 10 mL of DI water for 30 min, followed by the gradual addition of 10 mmol ZrCl4. The obtained solution was stirred for 15 min and transferred to a Teflon-lined flask to react at 120 °C for 4 h. The separation, washing, and drying processes were repeated as above, and the white residue was identified as MIP-202 NPs (Fig. 1 ).

His-GQDs NPs were prepared following the earlier reported thermal pyrolysis method with a slight change, as follows: 1 g L-His and 2 g CA were dissolved in 20 mL DI water, and the obtained solution was evaporated at 80 °C to dry completely. The solid that formed was subsequently subjected to heating at 180 °C for a duration of 2 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the mixture was gradually poured into a vigorously stirring 5 mg/mL NaOH solution (100 mL), in order to neutralize it. A solid sample was obtained by evaporating the solution containing His-GQD using a rotary [54]. The steps for synthesizing NPs are depicted in Fig. 1.

The porous PES substrates of FO membranes were prepared through the phase inversion technique [44,56]. Briefly, 2 g PES was dissolved in a mixed solution of 5.6 g PEG (as a pore former) and 8.4 g DMF (as a solvent). The prepared PES casting solution was kept at room temperature for 12 h to eliminate the entrapped air bubbles before being poured over a clean glass plate with a casting knife to achieve a thickness of 120 μm. The glass plate was soaked in a coagulation water bath at room temperature until phase inversion occurred. Finally, the solidified PES substrates were immersed in DI water for a minimum of 24 h before surface alterations. The schematic of the PES substrate preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 2a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TFC and TFN FO membranes fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic illustration of the employed process for developing TFC and NPs-PA TFN membranes is shown in Fig. 2b. To fabricate the TFC membrane, the top surfaces of the support layers were first exposed to an aqueous MPD solution (2 wt%) for 2 min. Then, the excess MPD solution was removed from the surface using N2 gas, and the same area was saturated with organic TMC solution (0.15 wt%) for 1 min. The resulting film was dried in an oven for 10 min at 60 °C to ensure completion of the polymerization reaction between the two monomers and the formation of a dense polyamide layer. The NPs-incorporated TFN membranes were prepared using the same protocol as the TFC membrane, but with varied quantities of MOF and His-GQD in the MPD solution. Different formulations of the fabricated TFN membranes are summarized in Supplementary Table S1 online. All prepared FO membranes were submerged in fresh DI water overnight before conducting FO tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FO performance assessment of the fabricated membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The experimental evaluation of the membranes' performances was carried out by a lab-scale FO setup [57]. Two gear pumps were employed to circulate the draw and feed solution (DS and FS, respectively) in opposite directions across the membrane surface at a flow rate of 0.2 L/min. Deionized water and sodium chloride solution at different concentrations from 0.5 to 2 M were used as FS and DS, respectively. All the fabricated membranes underwent testing in two different configurations: FO mode (active PA layer facing FS, AL-FS) and pressure retardant osmosis (PRO) mode (support layer facing FS, AL-DS). A calibrated conductivity meter was also utilized to gauge the changes in salt concentration in the FS. The FO water flux J
w (Lm−2 h−1, commonly referred to as LMH) and reverse solute flux J
s (gm−2 h−1, also referred to as gMH) were calculated by measuring the weight change of DS and salt concentration change of FS over a pre-determined period (half an hour). These two parameters were calculated using the following flux equations:

The water permeability coefficient (A, Lm−2h−1bar−1), the solute permeability coefficient (B, Lm−2 h−1), and the structural parameter (S, μm) of the prepared membranes were calculated through a four-step single FO experiment [58]. In this protocol, the J

To assess the antifouling trait of both the optimal TFN-MQ2 and control TFC membranes, BSA was used as a model foulant. The experiment was accomplished under AL-FS mode at ambient temperature. Prior to commencing the test, DI water was circulated through both the feed and draw sides for 15 min to wash the membrane surfaces and water channels. DI and NaCl (0.5–2 M) aqueous solutions were then applied as FS and DS, respectively, to regulate the membrane's initial water flux to ∼18 LMH. To counteract the impact of diluted DS in the experiment, the water flux was calculated every 30 min for 5 h. This procedure was repeated with FS containing 250 ppm of BSA fouling agent until fouling started and the water flux stabilized. Afterward, the membrane underwent a washing process where DI water was circulated at a higher flow rate of 400 mL/min for 30 min to remove adhered foulants from the membrane surface (physical cleaning method). Finally, the experiment continued with conditions almost identical to the baseline water flux determination step, and the water fluxes were measured. Heavy metal ions rejection ability of the TFN-M, TFN-Q, TFN-MQ2, and TFC membranes was evaluated using the same FO setup in the AL-FS orientation. In this study, aqueous solutions of the Pb2+ and Cu2+ with a concentration of 2000 ppm and 1 M NaCl were employed as FS and DS, respectively. Having completed the test, the concentration of targeted HM ions in the DS was measured utilizing inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES), and the rejection value (R

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MIP-202(Zr) and his-GQDs NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectral analysis was put into service to elucidate the chemical structure of the synthesized NPs, and the corresponding spectra are depicted in Fig. 3a. The FTIR spectrum of His-GQD displays several index peaks at wavenumbers of ∼3400, ∼2920, 1587 and 1397 cm−1 relating to the combined symmetric stretching vibrations of NH2, OH and CO2H groups, ν s (CH2), νs (CO) of carboxylate, and νs (CH), respectively [54,59]. Moreover, a pronounced peak at a wavenumber of 1704 cm−1 suggests the existence of carboxylic acid functional groups in this compound. The peaks detected at ∼3300, 1611, and 1436 cm−1 in the MIP-202(Zr) spectrum are linked to the vibrations of ν (NH), νas (CO) and νs (CO) of the linker, respectively [60,61]. Comparison of FTIR spectra of L-Asp and MIP-202(Zr) shows Zr–OH and Zr–COO peaks at 650 and 487 cm−1 and CO band shift from 1687 to 1611 cm−1, confirming successful synthesis of MIP-202(Zr) NPs [60,62,63]. The slight peak at 1720 cm−1 originates from the CO2H groups in the MOF, indicating that some carboxylate groups of the linker are not coordinated with the metal center. This aligns with the lower crystalline structure seen in Fig. 3b.

In Fig. 3b, the XRD spectra of His-GQD are compared to those of synthesized and simulated MIP-202(Zr). The His-GQD NPs exhibit only one broad diffraction peak from 20 to 40°, suggesting their amorphous nature [64,65]. The peaks observed at 8.5°, 9.9°, 20°, and 21.8° are assigned to (111), (200), (420), and (440) planes of the simulated MIP-202(Zr) pattern [61]. The XRD pattern of synthesized MIP-202(Zr) is overall in fair agreement with the simulated pattern, verifying the successful synthesis of the MIP-202(Zr) nanoparticles. However, the slight broadening of the synthesized MIP-202(Zr) peaks implies lower crystallinity in their structure.

The morphology and chemical composition of the prepared MOFs were studied using FE-SEM and EDX elemental analysis, with findings illustrated in Fig. 3e and f. As shown in Fig. 3e, MIP-202(Zr) NPs have aggregated spherical-like morphology with a mean particle size of ∼150–200 nm. The EDX elemental spectrum shows the constituent elements of these nanoparticles with approximate percentages well. As previously reported, owing to the presence of NH3 + groups in the MOF structure, the chlorine peak is also discernible in the MIP-202 spectrum [47,61]. This observation concluded from the high interaction between Cl− and NH3 + ions and reveals the ineffectiveness of the washing process in eliminating Cl− ions from MOF pores. The particle size distribution of as-prepared MIP-202 and His-GQDs in an aqueous suspension was investigated using the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique. The MIP-202 and His-GQDs nanoparticles had an average size of around 160.8 nm and 6.7 nm, respectively, as demonstrated in Fig. 3c and d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared FO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: ATR-FTIR analysis was conducted to examine the chemical structure of the prepared membranes (Fig. 4a). The observed bands at 1150, 1240, 1410, 1485, and 1580 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric stretching vibration of SO2, the asymmetric stretching of C–O–C, CC benzene ring stretching, CC stretching vibration and aromatic in-plane ring stretching vibration, respectively, and belongs to the PES support layer [38,66]. The polyamide layer formation evidence can be observed in the wavenumbers of 1650 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration) and 1550 cm−1 (NH bending vibration), ubiquitous in all samples [67]. Comparison of TFC and TFN (at high MOF loading) spectra discloses the appearance of two weak peaks at 1610 and 1714 cm−1, which may be linked to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of MOF carboxylate and CO2H groups on NPs and membrane surfaces, respectively. The carboxylic acid peak probably originated from the following sources: 1) the incomplete reaction of TMC with aqueous monomer, caused by the steric hindrance formed by NPs along the diffusion path of MPD molecules towards the polymerization zone. As a result, the number of unreacted acyl halide groups rises, leading to the potential hydrolysis into acid in an aqueous environment [56,68]. 2) Hydrolysis of the formed anhydride bonds between the NPs and TMC‌ during the IP step [69]. 3) The acidic groups present on the NPs. However, the consistent presence of His-GQDs in all samples and the absence of this peak in the TFN-MQ1 spectrum suggest that the desired peak at increased loading values of MIP-202 can be assigned to its acidic groups, which aligns with the MIP-202 FTIR results. These findings validate the successful incorporation of NPs within the PA selective layer. More detailed spectra are provided in Supplementary Fig. S1 online.

Fig. 4b depicts the XRD patterns of the control TFC and modified TFN membranes. The broad diffraction peak ranging from 10 to 25° (2 theta) seen in all patterns is caused by the amorphous nature of polymeric membranes [44,66]. Owning to a low concentration of nanofillers in the active layer of the TFN-MQ1 and TFN-MQ2 membranes, the MOF NPs' characteristic peaks cannot be identified in their corresponding spectra. By increasing the MOF loading percentage, one weak and broad peak at around 2 theta 9.3° is observed in both TFN-MQ3 and TFN-MQ4 spectra, originating from the combination of (111) and (200) planes of the MOF crystals. This observation further proves the triumphant incorporation of MOF NPs in the PA rejection structure.

The surface roughness data of all fabricated membranes containing Ra (roughness average), Rq (root mean square roughness), and Rm (the peak to valley roughness) are compiled in Table 1. Raising the NPs concentration in the active PA layer reduces all these parameters compared to the virgin TFC membrane. The Ra, Rq and Rm/10 values have been reduced from 73.3, 92.7 and 69.9 nm in TFC membrane to 50.0, 65.1 and 55.6 nm in TFN-MQ4. These results indicate that incorporating nanofillers at the PA layer smoothens the modified membranes' surface. Based on earlier studies, more deep valleys in rough membrane surfaces increase the foulant accumulation possibility in these places. Hence, the fabricated TFN membranes are anticipated to experience less significant fouling due to their lower susceptibility to foulant adsorption [73].

The surface topography and morphology of the membrane samples were characterized by SEM and AFM‌ analysis, and the resulting images are depicted in Fig. 5. The typical ridge-and-valley or flower-like morphology of the resulting PA layer is evident in all samples, verifying the successful implementation of the polymerization process between two monomers (top surface SEM images) [39]. As can be seen in these images, the elevated peaks in the PA layer of the TFC membrane are transformed into smaller ones as NPs enter, and there are hardly any high-altitude peaks in the TFN-MQ4 membrane. This finding aligns with AFM data (reduced ridge height in 3D images and lowered roughness parameters in Table 1), indicating that the presence of NPs leads to more even membrane surfaces. The possible reason for this event is the reduction in the IP reaction rate between two monomers because of the steric effects of NPs and hydrogen bonding interaction between the MPD amine groups and NPs functional groups. The membrane's smooth surface helps prevent fouling by reducing the chances of foulant species depositing in less rough areas, and any deposits can be easily removed during the physical cleaning step. EDX elemental analysis was employed to investigate the elemental composition of these samples; the corresponding spectra are illustrated in Supplementary Fig. S2 online. The presence of N and Zr peaks in EDS spectra indicates the successful creation of the PA layer (in modified samples, nanofillers may also contribute to this) and the embedding of MIP-202 NPs in this layer, respectively. It is important to highlight that as the MOF loading content rises, both the weight percentage of Zr and its peak intensity also rise. The data for TFN-M and TFN-Q membrane characterization can be found in Supplementary Fig. S3 online.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results for deionized water feed solution
ce:section.ce:para:
The FO salt/water separation performance of all prepared membranes was investigated using a lab-scale FO setup at room temperature. The water flux and reverse salt flux results of the TFN-Q and TFN-M membranes at a 1 M NaCl as DS and DI water as FS under FO mode are provided in Supplementary Fig. S4 online. As demonstrated, with the introduction of His-GQDs NPs (TFN-Q membrane) at the selective PA layer, the J
w value increases from 13.9 LMH in TFC membrane to 17.5 LMH. In another modified membrane with MOF NPs (TFN-M), the water flux is enhanced to 18.5 LMH. The rise in water flux caused by NPs may be explained by the hydrophilic functional groups of His-GQDs and MOF, including carboxylic acid, hydroxy, and amino groups (in line with FTIR data, Fig. 3a). The additional channels located between the introduced NPs (His-GQDs sheets and MOF NPs) and the PA selective layer are also an important factor as shown in previous studies involving sheets (e.g., GO, LDH, transition metal dichalcogenides, and synthetic talc) and 3D nanoparticles (such as MOFs) [49,74–77]. Besides, the porous nature of the MOF framework offers new pathways for water to flow through, leading to increased water flux in TFN-M compared to membrane modified with non-porous His-GQDs (TFN-Q). Compared to the virgin TFC membrane, the reverse solute flux values show a slight decrease in both modified membranes (TFN-M and TFN-Q). As expected, the reduction in the J
s value of TFN-M is more noticeable. This is a result of the excellent molecular sieving capability of the MOF, which has a pore window size ranging from approximately 4 to 5.5 Å, effectively blocking Na+ (7.16 Å) and Cl− hydrated ions (6.64 Å) [47]. The protonated form of the linkers amino group (i.e., NH3 +) is another possible factor (in agreement with EDX elemental and FTIR spectrum of MIP-202), which can increase MOF-modified membranes resistance to salt passage by repelling the same charged Na+ ions [47,61]. Subsequently, according to electroneutrality requirements, the penetration of the hydrated counter ions (i.e., Cl−) is likewise diminished.

Fig. 6 presents the FO performance results of the MOF/His-GQDs-modified membranes regarding J
w , J
s , and J
w /J
s values. Based on the results of chart a, all fabricated TFN membranes, with a constant amount of His-GQDs and various amounts of MOF loading, demonstrate higher J
w than unmodified TFC membranes in both tested modes. The improvement in water flux with a rise in MOF dosage content preserves to 0.100 wt% (TFN-MQ2). Enhanced water flux with the highest value of 21.8 LMH regards the TFN-MQ2 membrane, surpassing the control TFC by more than 1.5 times. As aforementioned, the rise in J
w could be related to the hydrophilic nature of the nanofillers, the highly porous structure of the MOF NPs, and the interfacial pores between NPs and PA. Owing to the presumable aggregation of MOF NPs and plugging some surface pores of the PES substrate layer at higher loading of MIP-202 (also evidenced by high S values, Table 2 ), the J
w values of the TFN-MQ3 and TFN-MQ4 membranes reduce gradually. While, the specific pore window size of MOF NPs restricts the transportation of the hydrated Na+ and Cl− ions through the membrane (the rejection PA layer), and the reverse salt flux constantly decreases by raising MOF loading (Fig. 6b). The calculated J
s /J
w ratios of the prepared membranes, as a parameter representing the selectivity of the FO membranes, are depicted in Fig. 6c. It is evident that the NPs-incorporated membranes show a lower J
s /J
w ratio than the pristine TFC membrane in both FO and PRO mode. Among modified membranes, the TFN-MQ2 membrane with the lowest J
s /J
w value demonstrates superior selectivity. Comparing the ion/water separation performance of TFN-Q, TFN-M, and TFN-MQ2 membranes reveals the superior water flux and selectivity of TFN-MQ2, attributed to the synergistic effect of His-GQDs and MIP-202 NPs. However, as stated earlier, due to surface pore blockage of the PES substrate layer due to MOF NPs accumulation, the synergistic effect weakens with increasing the MOF content (TFN-MQ3 and TFN-MQ4).

The top-performing TFN-MQ2 membrane was selected for its exceptional separation abilities in typical FO testing conditions, to assess its ion/water separation performance with varying DS concentrations. Following Fig. 7 , when the draw solution concentration is raised, the J
w value increases proportionally with the osmotic pressure disparity between the membrane's two sides. Similarly, a near-linear relation between the DS concentration and J
s was also noted because of the increased salt concentration gradient. It is important to stress that in every concentration of DS tested, the obtained J
w values for the optimal TFN-MQ2 membrane are significantly greater than those of the pristine TFC membrane. Meanwhile, the modified membrane can prevent salt penetration and detract the J
s values even when exposed to high concentrations of DS.

The structural parameter (S; the mass transport length scale across the support layer) and the water and solute permeability coefficients (A and B, respectively; the transport across the selective layer) of the fabricated membranes were determined via the FO-based protocol, and the findings are summarized in Table 2 [58]. The modified membranes were found to have higher A values than the virgin TFC membrane. The TFN-MQ2 membrane exhibited the highest water permeability improvement, but increasing the NPs content further gradually decreases the A parameter. As mentioned earlier, this immediate increase could be mainly attributed to the hydrogen bonding interactions formed between water molecules and the NPs functional groups (facial adsorption). Offering additional pathways for water molecules passage by nanofillers is another potential explanation. The secondary decrease may be linked to the blockage of support layer surface pores by NPs, which is supported by the high S values of TFN-MQ3 and TFN-MQ4 membranes (membranes with higher NPs loading). The NaCl permeability coefficient displays a trend similar to the A parameter, albeit with lower intensity. Similar studies have also shown that high A values are usually followed by high B values [38,78]. Nevertheless, the result favors the selectivity of the modified membrane (B/A ratio). The best-performing TFN-MQ2 membrane, with the A, B, and B/A ratio of 2.328 LMH bar−1, 0.358 LMH, and 0.154 bar, respectively, demonstrates superior separation efficiency than all other membranes fabricated membranes in this study. Apart from the exceptional values of A and B/A, it also possesses the smallest S parameter value (392 μm). In general, S changes proportionally with membrane thickness and tortuosity, but inversely with porosity, and can be calculated from experimental data as described in section 3.2. Hence, it seems that the introduction of MOF NPs has marginally reduced the membrane's porosity by creating extra channels. Low values of the S parameter are very desirable for reducing the ICP phenomenon and gaining high A values, as evidenced by the data collected for the A parameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results for simulated seawater feed solution
ce:section.ce:para:
The effectiveness of the selected membranes for desalination application was evaluated using 2 M NaCl DS and 0.6 M NaCl as the simulated seawater and FS in both FO and PRO configurations. Fig. 8 depicts the forward osmosis water flux of TFC and TFN-MQ2 membranes used in seawater desalination. Comparing Fig. 8 to Fig. 7a, there was a notable drop in water flux of the composite membranes with increased feed NaCl concentration due to the reduced osmotic pressure difference across the membrane. However, the J
w value of the TFN-MQ2 membrane remained superior to the pristine TFC membrane in both configurations because of the characteristics delivered by nanoparticles for modified membranes (mentioned in section 4.3.1.).

The summary of the salt/water separation abilities of the MIP-202&His-GQDs-incorporated membrane in the present study with those FO membranes in the scientific literature are listed in Supplementary Table S2 online. From the data in this table, it can be inferred that the J
w value for the TFN-MQ2 membrane is comparable with those of the literature. However, due to the inclusion of MIP-202 NPs in the active layer, its J
s value is relatively lower. The notable point is that our developed membrane has a more excellent selectivity (low J
s /J
w value) compared to other membranes in the literature, except for Nylon 6,6. Due to the unavailability of the heavy metal rejection performance for other table-listed membranes, we could not make comparisons regarding this feature. However, the outstanding HM ions rejection capabilities of our TFN-MQ2 membrane could provide an extra advantage compared to the rest.

The effect of the incorporated MOF and His-GQD NPs on the fouling tendency of the modified TFN-MQ2 membrane was appraised via dynamic fouling experiments in the presence of BSA – a representative protein foulant. As evident from Fig. 9a, the water flux through the two tested membranes shows a decreasing trend over time. Compared to the pristine TFC membrane, the TFN-MQ2 membrane displays less water flux drop and has higher flux stability against BSA foulant, revealing its superior antifouling trait. The higher surface hydrophilicity and smoothness of the TFN-MQ2 membrane, induced by His-GQDs and MOF species on the membrane's selective layer, are two key factors that collaborate in its antifouling power enhancement. This implies that a barrier-thin layer of water covers the PA membrane skin, preventing organic hydrophobic foulants accumulation on the membrane surface [71,72]. In addition, the much smoother surface of the modified membrane reduces adhesion sites, impeding the deposition of foulant molecules at valley-shaped areas [73]. Following the 5-h experiment, the TFN-MQ2 membrane experienced a 20 % decrease in flux, whereas the TFC membrane exhibited a 33 % decline simultaneously. To measure the detachability of the BSA deposit, membranes were washed physically with DI water for 30 min. The findings showed that the NPs-incorporated membrane exhibits a mostly reversible fouling behavior, restoring the flux to its initial values post-cleaning (water flux value in the absence of foulant). The antifouling parameters calculated confirm the results shown in Fig. 9b, demonstrating a notably higher flux recovery ratio for the TFN-MQ2 membrane compared to the pristine TFC membrane.

Reducing the concentration of HM ions to WHO and EPA standard values through forward osmosis is crucial because of the efficient, cost-effective, simple, and eco-friendly nature of this treatment method. HM rejection performance of the TFN-MQ2, TFN-M, TFN-Q, and TFC membranes under FO configuration are summarized in Fig. 10 . It is evident that switching the feed solution from DI water to a 2000 ppm heavy metal salt solution (Pb2+ and Cu2+) results in a decrease in water fluxes due to the reduced driving force across the membranes. The concentrative external concentration polarization (ECP) phenomenon could be another potential factor for the decrease in the net osmotic driving force and the reduced fluxes. The nanomaterials' adsorption capacity, facilitated by their functional groups, can intensify concentrative ECP on the feed side by absorbing solute cations (Pb2+ and Cu2+) and increasing their concentration on the membrane surface. Based on the obtained results, NPs-incorporated membranes performed better than the control TFC in rejecting Pb2+ and Cu2+ ions – TFN-MQ2 achieved the highest performance with 98.51 % rejection of Cu2+ ions and 98.10 % rejection of Pb2+ ions among all samples tested. The observed enhancement in the HM ion rejection performance can be demonstrated through various chemical and physical mechanisms. The initial factor concerns the molecular sieving capabilities of the MIP-202 NPs; the smaller pores window size of this compound compared to the hydrated copper and lead ions (8.38 Å and 8.02 Å, respectively) decrease metal ions transportation by raising the tortuosity of the PA layer [6,79,80]. It is worth noting that His-GQDs NPs can also create a highly tortuous PA layer, which elongates the transfer path for HM ions to penetrate through the rejection layer (ions passage over the nanofillers lamellar structure) [80]. On the other hand, the capability of NPs functional groups to coordinate with metal ions (such as imidazole, amine, hydroxyl, and carboxylate) enhances the adsorption tendency of the TFN-MQ2 membrane, resulting in enhanced rejection performance for Cu2+ and Pb2+ [54,81,82]. The positively charged amines of the MOF linkers are another influential factor, capable of repelling the like-charged Cu2+ and Pb2+ ions, as outlined in section 4.3. However, due to the modification with only one of the individual nanofillers (MOF or His-GQDs), the mentioned factors have limited effectiveness in the TFN-M and TFN-Q membranes. Hence, these membranes evince lower R
m values compared to the TFN-MQ2 membrane. Although the rejection values for both ions were similar for each membrane (with a maximum difference of 0.4 % for TFN-MQ2), the larger hydration diameter of Cu2+ resulted in slightly higher size exclusion effects for Cu2+ ions across all membranes tested, leading to higher rejection of Cu2+ compared to Pb2+.

A comparison of rejection capacities for HM of several types of modified FO membranes is provided in Supplementary Table S3 online. Despite the higher concentration of the used HM solution (FS) in our tested condition compared to previous studies (except MIP-801), our developed membrane showed remarkable Cu2+ and Pb2+ rejection. Hence, alongside improving desalination performance, increasing HM rejection potential can be achieved by concurrently modifying FO membranes with layered compounds containing multiple functional groups (functionalized GQDs) and porous compounds with unique cavities structures (MOFs with smaller pore window size than the hydrated HM ions and positively charged functional group). It should be emphasized that the main advantages of the present study are the low-cost and eco-friendly nanoparticles utilized, features not typically found in comparable instances.

In summary, the new MOF&GQDs-incorporated TFN membranes were fabricated through an IP reaction between TMC and MPD monomers. The reasons behind selecting MIP-202(Zr) and His-GQDs are their better compatibility with the active PA layer, superior stability in water, diverse hydrophilic functional groups, effortless synthesis, and porous and layered structures. The inserted NPs, whose presence was verified by various analysis methods, significantly boosted the separation and antifouling performances of the modified membranes. The enhanced hydrophilicity and smoother surfaces of the NPs-incorporated membrane are involved in this improvement in salt/water separation performance, as the incorporation of NPs alters the selective PA layer's physicochemical properties positively. In a typical FO experiment condition with AL-FS orientation, the optimal TFN-MQ2 membrane improves J
w , J
s , and J
s /J
w values by approximately 57 %, 18 %, and 37 % each, compared to the virgin TFC membrane. By achieving the flow recovery ratio of 92 % in the fouling experiment, this membrane exhibits nearly reversible fouling behavior. Moreover, the modified TFN-MQ2 membrane with 98.5 % Cu2+ and 98.1 % Pb2+ rejection demonstrates outstanding capability in rejecting HM ions from water. This research reveals how inexpensive and eco-friendly NPs can enhance TFN FO membranes performance in various applications, offering a cost-effective solution to improve performance and decrease production expenses.
